km.Wales: វេលស៍, អូនីតេដគីងដូម

zh.Fermoy, Cork, Ireland: 愛爾蘭庫迪 科克費爾莫易
zh.Roch Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales: 威爾士布洛可爾羅克 卡素
zh.Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales: 英國威爾斯布洛可爾羅克
zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Thomas De La Roche, Baron Roche of Fermoy and Roch, Sheriff of Cork, MP於大約1250年出生在威爾士布洛可爾羅克 卡素時，他的父親John De La Roche31歲，母親Lady Maud Le Waleys Baroness Roche27歲。他至少有3個兒子和5個女兒。他去世於1314年在英國威爾斯布洛可爾羅克享年65歲，埋葬在英國威爾斯布洛可爾羅克。

zh-hans.Fermoy, Cork, Ireland: 爱尔兰庫迪 科克費爾莫易
zh-hans.Roch Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales: 威尔士布洛可爾羅克 卡素
zh-hans.Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales: 英国威尔斯布洛可爾羅克
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir Thomas De La Roche, Baron Roche of Fermoy and Roch, Sheriff of Cork, MP于大约1250年出生在威尔士布洛可爾羅克 卡素时，他的父亲John De La Roche31岁，母亲Lady Maud Le Waleys Baroness Roche27岁。他至少有3个儿子和5个女儿。他去世于1314年在英国威尔斯布洛可爾羅克享年65岁，埋葬在英国威尔斯布洛可爾羅克。

nameMeanings.name: Roche,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Aidan, Declan, Eamonn, Fergus, Fionnuala, Kevin Patrick, Liam.
Irish (Munster) and English (of Norman origin): variant of   Roach  . Compare   Roch  .

French: topographic name for someone who lived by a rocky outcrop or crag, from Old French roche ‘rock’. In North America, this surname is also a shortened form of the variant Laroche 2. Compare Deroche .

variants.name: Laroche
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Armand, Fernand, Normand, Pierre, Jacques, Laurent, Adrien, Cecile, Germain, Marcel.
French:
habitational name from any of numerous places in various parts of France named Laroche or La Roche, from Old French roche ‘rock’, with feminine definite article la.

variants.name: Larocque
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Armand, Fernand, Henri, Marcel, Normand, Valmore, Yves, Achille, Amie, Donat, Emile.

French: habitational name from La Ro(c)que, the name of several places in various parts of France, from a regional variant of roche ‘rock’ (see Roche , compare Laroche ), with feminine definite article la. Compare Laroque .

Altered form of southern French Roquebrune or of its variant Rocquebrune: habitational name from Ro(c)quebrune, the name of several places in the southern part of France. The surname Roquebrune is rare in France, while the variant Rocquebrune is extinct there. Compare Larock , Lerocque , Rockburn , and Rocque 1.

variants.name: Rochet
meanings: French: diminutive of   Roche  , a topographic name meaning ‘rock’, or a habitational name from (Le) Rochet, the name of several places in various parts of France.
Catalan: of French origin (see above). This surname is also found in the Dominican Republic and Puerto Rico.

variants.name: Rochat
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Monique, Philippe, Pierre.
French: topographic name from a diminutive of Old French roche ‘rock’ (see Roche 2), or a habitational name from Le Rochat, the name of several places in the eastern part of France.

variants.name: Roach
meanings: English:

of Norman origin, from Old French and Middle English roche ‘rock, cliff, promontory’, sometimes a translation of Stone . The surname may have been topographic, denoting someone who lived on or by a prominent rock or rocky place, or a habitational name for someone who lived or came from a place so named, such as one of those called Roque or Roche(s) in Normandy and neighboring regions, or Roche in Cornwall, Roach Farm in Clyst Hydon (Devon), or Roch in Pembrokeshire (see Roch ).

in Lancashire perhaps referring to the River Roch, which runs through Rochdale; the river name is probably a back-formation from Recedham, an early name for Rochdale recorded in 1086, derived from Old English reced ‘building, house, hall’ + hām ‘village, homestead’. Alternatively, the Lancashire name could be a shortened form of the now rare or extinct surname Rochdale alias Rachedale, Rachdall, a habitational name from Rochdale (Lancashire). From the 18th century onward the name is difficult to distinguish from Roch , borne by Irish migrants to South Lancashire, especially Liverpool.

variants.name: Roquet
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Camille.
French: topographic name from a diminutive of roche ‘rock’ (see Roche and Roque ), or a habitational name from (Le) Roquet, the name of several places in various parts of France.

variants.name: Rochez
meanings: Walloon and French: variant of   Roche   2 or   Rocher  .

nameMeanings.name: Baron
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French, English, Dutch, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and Croatian; Breton (also Le Baron): from Old French barun, baron, Middle English barun, baroun, Middle Dutch baroen, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and dialectal Croatian baron ‘baron’ (i.e. a nobleman ranking below an earl and above a knight), a title of nobility which was in England also awarded to certain freemen of the cities of London and York and of the Cinque Ports. As a surname it is unlikely to be a status name denoting a person of rank. Generally, it referred to service in a baronial household or was acquired as a nickname by a peasant who had ideas above his station or applied to a man showing characteristics of a nobleman. See also 3 and 4 below, compare Barron and Lebaron .
French: habitational name from (Le) Baron, the name of several places in various parts of France. Compare   Lebaron  .
German: status name for a freeman or baron, from barūn ‘imperial or church official’, a loanword in Middle High German from Old French (see 1 above).

variants.name: Barron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Jesus, Manuel, Francisco, Carlos, Miguel, Raul, Pedro, Ruben, Alfredo, Luis.

Scottish: status name from Scots baron, which in the Highlands denoted a small proprietary landholder. In Angus it alluded specifically to a tenant of one of the small baronies attached to the Abbey of Coupar Angus. The surname was taken by Scottish migrants to Ulster. Compare Baron .
Scottish: shortened form of McBarron, from Scottish Gaelic Mac a' Bharain ‘son of the baron’. See Baron and 1 above.

variants.name: Boron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Krystyna, Edyta, Leszek, Mieczyslaw, Mieczyslawa, Wladyslaw.
Polish (Boroń): from a pet form of the personal names Borzysław or Borzymir, formed with the element bor ‘to fight’ (from Old Slavic boriti se).
French: habitational name from a place so called in Franche-Comté.

variants.name: Baro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Mario, Manuel, Ana, Angel, Carlos, Cesar, Fernando, Jesus, Jose, Orlando, Ramon, Rosalia.  Italian Guido, Angelo, Carmelo, Federico, Lucio.
Catalan (Baró): status name from baró ‘free man, baron’ (see Baron 1), or a habitational name from a place so named.
Hungarian (Báró): status name from báró ‘baron’. In the form Baró, it could be from a pet form of the personal names Barabás (see Barabas ) or Bertalan.

variants.name: Barona
meanings: Spanish: from the feminine form of Barón ‘baron’ (see Baron ), applied as a nickname or as an occupational name for a member of the household of a baroness.
Italian: probably from the feminine form of   Barone  . The name is concentrated in Sondrio province (Lombardy).

variants.name: Barren
meanings: English: probably a variant of   Baron   or   Barran  .

nameMeanings.name: Roche
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Aidan, Declan, Eamonn, Fergus, Fionnuala, Kevin Patrick, Liam.
Irish (Munster) and English (of Norman origin): variant of   Roach  . Compare   Roch  .

French: topographic name for someone who lived by a rocky outcrop or crag, from Old French roche ‘rock’. In North America, this surname is also a shortened form of the variant Laroche 2. Compare Deroche .

variants.name: Laroche
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Armand, Fernand, Normand, Pierre, Jacques, Laurent, Adrien, Cecile, Germain, Marcel.
French:
habitational name from any of numerous places in various parts of France named Laroche or La Roche, from Old French roche ‘rock’, with feminine definite article la.

variants.name: Larocque
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Armand, Fernand, Henri, Marcel, Normand, Valmore, Yves, Achille, Amie, Donat, Emile.

French: habitational name from La Ro(c)que, the name of several places in various parts of France, from a regional variant of roche ‘rock’ (see Roche , compare Laroche ), with feminine definite article la. Compare Laroque .

Altered form of southern French Roquebrune or of its variant Rocquebrune: habitational name from Ro(c)quebrune, the name of several places in the southern part of France. The surname Roquebrune is rare in France, while the variant Rocquebrune is extinct there. Compare Larock , Lerocque , Rockburn , and Rocque 1.

variants.name: Rochet
meanings: French: diminutive of   Roche  , a topographic name meaning ‘rock’, or a habitational name from (Le) Rochet, the name of several places in various parts of France.
Catalan: of French origin (see above). This surname is also found in the Dominican Republic and Puerto Rico.

variants.name: Rochat
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Monique, Philippe, Pierre.
French: topographic name from a diminutive of Old French roche ‘rock’ (see Roche 2), or a habitational name from Le Rochat, the name of several places in the eastern part of France.

variants.name: Roach
meanings: English:

of Norman origin, from Old French and Middle English roche ‘rock, cliff, promontory’, sometimes a translation of Stone . The surname may have been topographic, denoting someone who lived on or by a prominent rock or rocky place, or a habitational name for someone who lived or came from a place so named, such as one of those called Roque or Roche(s) in Normandy and neighboring regions, or Roche in Cornwall, Roach Farm in Clyst Hydon (Devon), or Roch in Pembrokeshire (see Roch ).

in Lancashire perhaps referring to the River Roch, which runs through Rochdale; the river name is probably a back-formation from Recedham, an early name for Rochdale recorded in 1086, derived from Old English reced ‘building, house, hall’ + hām ‘village, homestead’. Alternatively, the Lancashire name could be a shortened form of the now rare or extinct surname Rochdale alias Rachedale, Rachdall, a habitational name from Rochdale (Lancashire). From the 18th century onward the name is difficult to distinguish from Roch , borne by Irish migrants to South Lancashire, especially Liverpool.

variants.name: Roquet
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Camille.
French: topographic name from a diminutive of roche ‘rock’ (see Roche and Roque ), or a habitational name from (Le) Roquet, the name of several places in various parts of France.

variants.name: Rochez
meanings: Walloon and French: variant of   Roche   2 or   Rocher  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sung, Jung, Yong, Chang, Kwang, Sang, Dong, Hyun, Jae, Jin, Kyung, Jong, Chong, Chung, Byung, Jeong, Pyong, Myong, Hak, Jin Hwan, Sook, Yeong, Choon.
Korean: variant of   O  .
Japanese: variant of Ō (see O ).

variants.name: O
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Bo Young, Chae, Chong Hwan, Dong Kun, Ho, Hwan, Hyun, Jong, Jung, Ki Soon, Kwang.  Korean Min, Chong, Chol, Jung Ho, Myong, Sa, Sung Kyun.

Korean (오; Chinese character: 吳): historically, there were two Chinese characters for the O surname, but by the late Koryŏ period (14th century AD ), one of them had disappeared. Some records indicate that there are as many as 210 different clans for the remaining character, but only sixteen can be positively documented. All sixteen of these clans originate from the same ancestor, O Ch’ŏm (吳 瞻), who migrated to Korea from China during the reign of Shilla's King Chijŭng (500–514 AD ). O is a common surname throughout Korea. Compare Oh .

nameMeanings.name: Fermoy
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Italian:
habitational name from Fermo (Marche).
in some instances, from the personal name Fermo.

nameMeanings.name: and
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of French Canadian Hénault or directly of French Hunault (see Henault 2).
Catalan (Añó): Castilianized form of Anyó, probably from the old personal name Annone.
Japanese: variant of   Anno  .

variants.name: Anno
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Japanese Masayuki, Tadashi, Yoshito.
Japanese: written 安野 ‘peaceful plain’. This surname is rare in Japan proper, and may have originated in the Ryūkyū Islands, where it is pronounced   Ano  .

variants.name: Henault
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Andre, Emile, Lucien, Martial, Pierre, Camille, Fernand, Laurier, Monique, Normand, Philippe.
French (Hénault): from the ancient Germanic personal name Haginwald, composed of the elements hagin ‘enclosure, protected place’ and wald ‘power, authority’.
Altered form (Hénault) of French Hunault: from the ancient Germanic personal name Hunwald, composed of the elements hun ‘bear cub’ and wald ‘power, authority’. Compare Ano and Eno .

nameMeanings.name: Roch,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Jacques, Emile, Gilles, Jean-Marc, Julien, Marcel, Michel, Pierre, Thierry, Yves.

French, German, Sorbian, Polish, and Czech: from the personal name Roch, Latin Rochus, of ancient Germanic origin, derived from Hrocho, a short form of the compound names with the first element hrok ‘crow’. It may have also been originally a byname meaning ‘crow’. The name was fairly common in the Middle Ages, bestowed in honor of a 14th-century Christian saint Rochus of Montpellier, remembered for his miraculous healings during an outbreak of the plague in northern Italy.
German and Czech: from a short form of ancient Germanic compound names like Rochold, related to 1 above.

variants.name: Rochel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Fidel, Ignacio, Maria Luisa, Porfirio, Refugio, Salvador.
German: from a pet form of the personal name   Roch   or   Roche  . See also 2 below.
French (Alsace, Lorraine, and Vosges): possibly a derivative of   Roche   ‘rock’. Alternatively, of German origin; see 1 above.

variants.name: Roque
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Manuel, Pedro, Jesus, Francisco, Carlos, Mario, Luis, Ricardo, Jorge, Julio.
Spanish and Portuguese: from the personal name Roque (see Roch ).
Spanish: habitational name from any of the places in Gran Canaria called Roque or from El Roque in Tenerife.

variants.name: Rocco
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Carmine, Pasquale, Antonio, Dino, Carlo, Domenic, Gennaro, Giacomo, Vito, Aldo.
Italian: from the personal name Rocco, a short form of the ancient Germanic compound names with the first element hrok ‘crow’ (see Roch 1).

variants.name: St. Rock
meanings: Americanized form of French Saint-Roch: habitational name from a parish in Paris and several other places in various parts of France named Saint-Roch (from the dedication of their churches to Saint Rochus), or a nickname with the prefix Saint as an (ironic) attachment to the personal name or surname Roch . — Note: The population figure published by the US Census Bureau does not distinguish between the surnames St. Rock and Strock .
History: Jacques Lagarde dit Saint-Roch from Paris, France, married Marie-Catherine Lafargue in Montreal, QC, in 1749.

variants.name: Rod
meanings: German: habitational name from any of several places called Rod, or a topographic name for somene who lived in a woodland clearing, from a derivative of Middle High German roden ‘to clear land for cultivation’. Compare Rhodes .
Norwegian (Rød): habitational name from any of numerous farmsteads so named, from Old Norse ruth ‘clearing’.
Czech and Croatian: from a short form of the Slavic compound personal names based on the element rod ‘family, relatives’, e.g. Rodoslav.

variants.name: Roc
meanings: Catalan: from the personal name Roc (see Roch 1).
English: variant of   Rock  .

variants.name: Roh
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Jacques, Emile, Gilles, Jean-Marc, Julien, Marcel, Michel, Pierre, Thierry, Yves.

French, German, Sorbian, Polish, and Czech: from the personal name Roch, Latin Rochus, of ancient Germanic origin, derived from Hrocho, a short form of the compound names with the first element hrok ‘crow’. It may have also been originally a byname meaning ‘crow’. The name was fairly common in the Middle Ages, bestowed in honor of a 14th-century Christian saint Rochus of Montpellier, remembered for his miraculous healings during an outbreak of the plague in northern Italy.
German and Czech: from a short form of ancient Germanic compound names like Rochold, related to 1 above.

variants.name: Rohe
meanings: North German (also Röhe): from a short form of an ancient Germanic personal name formed with Middle High German rōhen ‘to roar’ or Old High German ruoh ‘care, intent’ (see Roch ).

nameMeanings.name: Sheriff
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish: occupational name or nickname from Middle English shirreve, sher(r)eve, sheref(fe) ‘sheriff, officer of the Crown’ (Old English scīrgerēfa, from scīr ‘shire, county’ + (ge)rēfa ‘reeve’), literally the reeve (i.e. steward) of a shire. Compare Reeve .
Muslim (mainly Nigeria, Sierra Leone, and Liberia): variant of   Sharif  . In Sierra Leone and Liberia this name is found mainly among the Mandinka people. Compare   Sirleaf  .

variants.name: Sirleaf
meanings: West African (Liberia): altered form of the predominantly Mandinka Muslim name   Sheriff  .

variants.name: Reeve
meanings: English (East Anglia): occupational name from Middle English reve, refe ‘reeve’ (Old English gerēfa). In medieval England a reeve was an official responsible for the administration of a manor, including organizing work done by the peasants on the land for their lord, collecting rents, selling produce, and so on.
Irish: variant of   Reeves   5.

variants.name: Shreve
meanings: English (Norfolk): variant of   Sheriff  . Compare   Shreeve  ,   Shreves  .

variants.name: Sherriff
meanings: English: variant of   Sheriff  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sung, Jung, Yong, Chang, Kwang, Sang, Dong, Hyun, Jae, Jin, Kyung, Jong, Chong, Chung, Byung, Jeong, Pyong, Myong, Hak, Jin Hwan, Sook, Yeong, Choon.
Korean: variant of   O  .
Japanese: variant of Ō (see O ).

variants.name: O
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Bo Young, Chae, Chong Hwan, Dong Kun, Ho, Hwan, Hyun, Jong, Jung, Ki Soon, Kwang.  Korean Min, Chong, Chol, Jung Ho, Myong, Sa, Sung Kyun.

Korean (오; Chinese character: 吳): historically, there were two Chinese characters for the O surname, but by the late Koryŏ period (14th century AD ), one of them had disappeared. Some records indicate that there are as many as 210 different clans for the remaining character, but only sixteen can be positively documented. All sixteen of these clans originate from the same ancestor, O Ch’ŏm (吳 瞻), who migrated to Korea from China during the reign of Shilla's King Chijŭng (500–514 AD ). O is a common surname throughout Korea. Compare Oh .

nameMeanings.name: Cork,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Irish, English: habitational name from Cork (Ireland).
English: nickname from Middle English cork ‘cork’ (of Celtic origin), perhaps metonymic for a supplier of red or purple dye made from cork, or for a dyer of cloth; compare Corker ).

variants.name: Corker
meanings: English: occupational name from an agent derivative of Middle English cork ‘purple or red dyestuff’, denoting someone who prepared or sold dyes.

variants.name: Corke
meanings: English (southern): variant of   Cork  .

nameMeanings.name: MP
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Chung, Chang, Jian, Li, Tong, Bin, Chao, Cheng, Chin, Chuan, Der, Feng, Fung, Hua.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 穆, meaning ‘solemnity, virtue’: (i) from Mu (穆), posthumous title of the Duke Mu of Song (died 720  BC ), the 14th ruler of the state of Song (located in present-day Shangqiu in Henan province). He earned the title Mu, 穆, because of his virtue during his reign. (ii) from the Qiu Mu Ling (丘穆陵) family, members of the Xianbei ethnic group in ancient northern China, who changed their original surname to Mu (穆) during the Northern Wei dynasty (386–534  AD ). (iii) from Mu (穆), posthumous title of the Duke Mu of Lu (died 377  BC ), ruler of the state of Lu (located in present-day Shandong province).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 慕: (i) said to be traced back to Yu Mu (虞慕), ancestor of the legendary Emperor Shun (c. 23rd century BC ). (ii) shortened form of the compound surname Mu-Rong (慕容), a surname from the Xianbei and Tuyuhun ethnic group in northern China. (iii) a surname from Korean and Jurchen ethnic groups in northeastern China.

variants.name: Moo
meanings: Chinese: variant of   Mu  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 巫, possibly based on its Hakka pronunciation, see   Wu   4.
Burmese (Karen): variant of   Mu   9.

variants.name: Wu
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Ming, Wei, Jian, Mei, Jing, Ying, Bin, Hong, Jin, Ping, Ching, Wen.
Chinese:

Mandarin form of the surname 吳, meaning ‘big, large’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from Wu (吳), the name of a state (located in present-day Jiangsu province) which was granted to Tai Bo, the first son of King Tai of Zhou (died c. 1146 BC ). After it was annexed by the state of Yue in 473 BC , people from the state adopted 吳 (Wu) as their surname. (ii) from the placename Yu (虞) (located in present-day Shanxi province). Because Yu (虞) had a similar pronunciation to that of Wu (吳) in ancient Chinese, some people acquired Wu (吳) instead of Yu (虞) as their surname.

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of Simon , now rare, but more common in the Middle Ages, when it gave rise to the surnames Simms and Simpson.

variants.name: Simon
meanings: Usual English form of Simeon , borne in the New Testament by various characters: two apostles, a brother of Jesus, a Pharisee, a leper, a tanner, a sorcerer (who offered money for the gifts of the Holy Ghost, giving rise to the term simony), and the man who carried Jesus's cross to the Crucifixion.

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of any of the given names beginning with the letter D- ( compare Jay and Kay ), in particular Dorothy . It is also used as an independent name, and may in some cases be associated with the River Dee ( compare Clyde ).

nameMeanings.name: La
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: ‘Orchid’.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Thomas De La Roche, Baron Roche of Fermoy and Roch, Sheriff of Cork, MP was born about 1250, in Roch Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales, his father, John De La Roche, was 31 and his mother, Lady Maud Le Waleys Baroness Roche, was 27. He had at least 3 sons and 5 daughters. He died in 1314, in Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom, at the age of 65, and was buried in Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom.

fr.Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales: Roch, Pembrokeshire, Pays de Galles, Royaume-Uni
fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Thomas De La Roche, Baron Roche of Fermoy and Roch, Sheriff of Cork, MP est né environ 1250, à Roch Castle, Pembrokeshire, Galles, son père, John De La Roche, avait 31 ans et sa mère, Lady Maud Le Waleys Baroness Roche, avait 27 ans. Il a eu au moins 3 fils et 5 filles. Il est décédé en 1314, à Roch, Pembrokeshire, Pays de Galles, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 65 ans, et a été enterré à Roch, Pembrokeshire, Pays de Galles, Royaume-Uni.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir Thomas De La Roche, Baron Roche of Fermoy and Roch, Sheriff of Cork, MP um 1250 in Roch Castle, Pembrokeshire, Wales geboren wurde, war sein Vater John De La Roche 31 und seine Mutter Lady Maud Le Waleys Baroness Roche 27. Er hatte mindestens 3 Söhne und 5 Töchter. Er starb 1314 im Alter von 65 Jahren in Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom und wurde in Roch, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom begraben.
